rule: Every required pin on an instantiated component must connect to at least one net.
explanation: Components carry a per-pin contract that flags pins as required when leaving them open would create a deterministic failure: a USB-C jack with no GND, an MCU whose reset pin is unwired, a sensor whose I2C lines float. Schema validation alone cannot catch this — the validator does not know which pins matter for which component. The structural check S1 inspects the net graph and reports each required pin that has no membership in any net.
heuristic: If you intentionally left a required pin unconnected (for example, an EN/RESET pin that you wire up later from a header), set the component's `required: false` override on that pin in the local profile copy. This is a starting point — for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: A net with only one endpoint is incomplete — every wire needs a sender and a receiver.
explanation: A net listing exactly one pin is almost always a typo: the second endpoint was forgotten, or the cleanup of a refactor left a stub. A one-endpoint net has no defined electrical behaviour — the would-be signal goes nowhere — so the renderer and exporters cannot meaningfully include it. S2 flags it as a wiring error.
heuristic: If you truly want an unconnected test point, declare it on the relevant component pin rather than as a free-standing net. As a starting point, consider whether the topology you describe matches the topology you intend. For precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: Two `net:` entries with the same name are a name collision unless an explicit merge is intended.
explanation: YAML accepts duplicate `net:` keys silently — both entries land in the parsed graph and the merge resolution depends on declaration order. That is rarely what the author meant. The duplicate is usually a copy-paste artefact or a rename that did not finish. S3 surfaces it as a warning so the author can choose to rename one side or fold the two definitions into one.
heuristic: Pick distinct names per logical net. If you genuinely want two halves of one net (e.g. a split rail joined later), declare them under one name with a `pins:` form listing every endpoint. This is a starting point — for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: Component references in connections must point at a component declared in the components map.
explanation: When a `connections` entry references `U1.D13` but no `U1` appears under `components`, the parser cannot resolve the pin. This is almost always a rename gap — the component was renamed and a stale reference remained. Schema validation catches it before the ERC engine runs; the catalog entry exists so the report writer can present every S/E finding through the same teaching path.
heuristic: Use your editor's project-wide rename when refactoring component refs so every reference moves together. As a starting point, check that every `REF.PIN` token in `connections` matches a key in `components`. For precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: Pin references in connections must name a pin that exists on the component's profile.
explanation: Pin names follow the silkscreen of the dev board (`D13`, `VBUS`, `GNDL`) and the profile in `circuitsmith.components` is the canonical list. A token like `U1.IO13` may match the silicon-level identifier but not the dev-board pin name; the schema validator flags it so the author can correct the reference. The catalog row exists so the report writer treats S5 findings the same way as predicate findings.
heuristic: Read the component's profile dictionary to confirm pin names — that is the source of truth, not the chip datasheet. The `alt:` field on each pin holds the silicon identifier when it differs. This is a starting point — for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: A signal input must have a defined idle level — either a firmware pull or a hardware pull resistor.
explanation: An MCU input pin that is neither driven nor pulled floats. Its measured voltage swings on capacitive coupling from neighbouring traces and the input reads as random bits. Pull-up or pull-down resistors define the idle state when no external source is driving the pin; firmware pulls (the chip's internal weak resistors enabled via `INPUT_PULLUP`) do the same job for inputs the firmware owns. A floating input is rarely the intent and almost always a bug.
heuristic: For a typical button-to-GND wired on an MCU GPIO, enable `INPUT_PULLUP` in firmware and declare `pull: firmware` on the net — that is the cheapest defined idle state. If the boot-time level matters (strapping pins, level-set inputs), use an external 10 kΩ pull-up to VCC or pull-down to GND. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: A standard LED always requires a current-limiting resistor in series.
explanation: An LED has very little internal resistance once its forward-voltage threshold is exceeded. Connecting it directly to an MCU GPIO sources excessive current — easily 50–100 mA, well above the chip's per-pin budget. The LED and the GPIO both fail, usually permanently. A series resistor caps the current at a safe level by absorbing the voltage difference between the supply and the LED's forward voltage.
heuristic: For a 3.3 V supply (ESP32, nRF52, Raspberry Pi), start with 220 Ω. For a 5 V supply (Arduino Uno, vintage TTL), start with 330 Ω. A higher value is always safer — the LED is dimmer but nothing breaks. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: An LED current-limiting resistor should land between roughly 100 Ω and 1 kΩ on a 3.3 V or 5 V supply.
explanation: Below ~100 Ω the resistor stops doing its job: the GPIO sources more current than the chip can sustain, the LED dies bright, and the pin's drive transistor weakens with every cycle. Above ~1 kΩ the LED becomes too dim to see in normal indoor light — the indicator stops indicating. E3 warns when a resistor value falls outside this safe window so the author can rerun the calculation or pick a different part.
heuristic: Start with 220 Ω on 3.3 V and 330 Ω on 5 V (standard red/green/blue LEDs). For very low-current LEDs (often labelled `LE` or `low-current`), 1 kΩ to 4.7 kΩ is acceptable and the LED specifies its rated forward current. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: An input-only pin must not share a net with any output-driving pin.
explanation: Some MCU pins are physically input-only — they have no output transistor and cannot drive the line. The ESP32 GPIO34–GPIO39 are the canonical example. Wiring such a pin to anything that drives the same net (another GPIO configured as output, a peripheral chip's output, an LED cathode) creates a contention. The input pin sees the driver's voltage instead of the intended signal, and reading the input returns whatever the driver decided. E4 catches this membership conflict.
heuristic: Restrict input-only pins to passive sensing roles: buttons, level-detect lines, ADC inputs, edge-triggered interrupts. If you need an output on the same logical line, pick a regular bidirectional GPIO instead. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: A strapping pin connected to a switch needs a defined idle level so boot is deterministic.
explanation: ESP32-class chips sample certain GPIOs at reset to select boot mode, flash voltage, and console verbosity. Those `is_strapping` pins must read a defined level when reset releases. A switch wired between a strapping pin and GND has no defined level when the switch is open — boot becomes a coin-flip between two modes. A pull (firmware or hardware) anchors the open-switch state to a known level.
heuristic: For ESP32 IO0/IO2/IO5/IO12/IO15 wired to a switch, declare `pull: firmware` on the net and call `pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)` in `setup()` before any other peripheral. If the switch is in line with the boot strap (e.g. IO0 driving boot mode), prefer an external 10 kΩ pull-up to VCC so the level holds even before firmware runs. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: Every active IC on a VCC rail needs a decoupling capacitor near its supply pin.
explanation: Digital ICs switch internal transistors at MHz rates. Each switch demands a brief current spike from VCC; the supply rail and the wires to it have inductance, and inductance fights sudden current changes by dropping voltage at the IC. A 100 nF ceramic capacitor across VCC and GND, placed within a few millimetres of the IC, supplies that spike locally — the rail keeps a stable voltage and the IC keeps switching cleanly. Skipping decoupling causes erratic resets, RF noise, and intermittent communication faults.
heuristic: Place one 100 nF ceramic capacitor across VCC and GND for every active IC, as close to the chip's power pins as the layout allows. Add one bulk 10 µF capacitor near the regulator output for low-frequency support. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: An I2C bus needs pull-up resistors on both SDA and SCL.
explanation: I2C is an open-drain bus: every device on the bus can pull the line low, but none of them drive it high. The lines float when no one is pulling — and a floating line reads as either 0 or 1 depending on stray capacitance, which destroys signalling. Pull-up resistors on SDA and SCL define the idle high level. Without them the bus does not communicate at all; you see ACK timeouts and bus-stuck conditions in firmware.
heuristic: Use 4.7 kΩ pull-ups to VCC on SDA and SCL for typical 100 kHz / 400 kHz busses with a few devices. Drop to 2.2 kΩ for fast-mode-plus (1 MHz) or longer cable runs. Many breakout boards integrate their own pull-ups — check the schematic before adding externals to avoid stacking too low. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: The summed current of every active LED must stay within the MCU's total-current budget.
explanation: Individual LED currents look small (5–20 mA each), but a status panel with eight indicators easily hits 100 mA across the chip. The MCU profile carries a `max_total_current_ma` figure — typically 200 mA on a 3.3 V dev board limited by the onboard regulator or the chip's combined I/O budget. Going above it browns the rail, the chip resets, and the failure is intermittent and hard to diagnose. E8 sums the indicator current at the YAML level so the budget overrun is caught before it ships.
heuristic: Keep the total LED current below half the chip's stated budget so you have headroom for other peripherals. For high-count indicator boards, drive LEDs from a separate transistor or LED-driver IC rather than directly from GPIOs. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: A power-input connector should have a series diode or P-MOSFET so reverse polarity does not kill the board.
explanation: A barrel jack inserted with the wrong polarity, a battery clipped on backwards, or a non-compliant USB-C cable that mis-asserts CC can deliver negative voltage to the board's supply rail. Without a protection element the MCU sees the reverse voltage on its substrate diodes and the chip dies within milliseconds. A Schottky diode in series with the input absorbs ~0.3 V at the cost of one diode drop; a P-MOSFET pulled to GND through its gate gives lower drop at higher currents. USB-C compliant cables mitigate this but not universally — protection at the board is the safer baseline.
heuristic: For 5 V USB-C or DC barrel inputs feeding a 3.3 V regulator, a 1N5819 Schottky in series with VBUS gives bullet-proof protection at minimal cost. For battery-powered designs at higher currents, a P-MOSFET with its gate tied to GND through a 100 kΩ pull-down is the next step up. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: The same physical pin cannot appear in more than one net.
explanation: A pin has one electrical state at a time. Listing `U1.D13` under both `BTN_A` and `BTN_B` describes a contradiction — the pin would have to belong to two distinct nets, which is impossible. The author either meant to merge the two nets (use one net name with both buttons) or to use two different pins. E10 catches the conflict before it becomes a runtime debug session.
heuristic: If multiple devices share a pin (e.g. an I2C bus), put them all in one net under a shared name. If you accidentally re-used a pin, pick a fresh GPIO from the MCU's `pins:` map. This is a starting point; for precision applications, calculate the exact value or consult the datasheet.

rule: Every declared port on a sub-block instance must appear in at least one top-level connection.
explanation: A sub-block exposes ports — `signal_in`, `gnd`, etc. — so the surrounding circuit can wire to it. If you declare a port and never reference `<instance>.<port>` anywhere in the top-level `connections:`, the port is electrically floating; the sub-block's promise to its internals is broken. E11 catches this before the layout kernel runs.
heuristic: Treat each port like a pin on a discrete component: it must connect to something. The fix is one of two things: wire the port to its intended destination, or remove the port from the sub-block definition if you didn't mean to expose it. This is a starting point; review the sub-block's intent before suppressing the warning.
source_of_truth: EPIC-014 frozen decisions — named-ports map (IDEA-008 Half 2).

rule: A sub-block defined in `sub-blocks:` should be referenced by at least one `instances:` entry.
explanation: A sub-block definition with no instances is dead code — the YAML parses but nothing in the circuit uses it. It is almost always a typo (wrong sub-block name in `instances.X.sub-block:`) or leftover work-in-progress. E12 is a warning, not an error, because there are legitimate reasons (libraries shared across circuits) but the v0.1 schema does not ship that pattern.
heuristic: Add an `instances:` entry referencing the sub-block, or remove the definition. Search the `instances:` block for the sub-block's key — the typo is usually a misspelling there. This is a starting point; if you keep the sub-block as a shared library template, suppress E12 via `meta.erc`.
source_of_truth: EPIC-014 frozen decisions — single bundled epic for sub-blocks.

rule: The flattener's minted refdes (`<local>_<instance>`) must not collide with any flat-form component refdes.
explanation: EPIC-014 mints global refdes by suffixing the local refdes with the instance name (e.g. `R` inside `FILT_A` becomes `R_FILT_A`). If you also declare a top-level component named `R_FILT_A`, the flattener can't tell which one a BOM row refers to. E13 catches this collision before the netgraph is built.
heuristic: Rename one of the colliding components. The minted refdes is a function of the sub-block + instance names, so renaming the instance (or the local refdes inside the sub-block) is usually simpler than renaming the flat component. This is a starting point; the underlying invariant (unique refdes across the flattened circuit) is non-negotiable.

rule: Each `<instance>.<port>` reference must appear in exactly one top-level net.
explanation: A sub-block port maps to a single internal pin. If two distinct top-level nets both reference `FILT_A.signal_in`, the flattener has to choose one and silently ignore the other — or worse, short-circuit two nets together. E14 catches this driver conflict before the netgraph hides it under one merged net.
heuristic: Pick one top-level net to drive the port. If you genuinely want two sources to share a node, name a single net and put both sources on it. This is a starting point; review the schematic intent before merging nets.

rule: An R+R rail-to-GND topology with a tap is treated as a voltage divider only when the tap-net name matches `^(V?REF|SENSE|ADC|DIV|TAP)` or one of the resistors carries `role: divider`.
explanation: Two resistors in series between a rail and ground look identical to a series-resistor on a current path with an incidental tap label. Without a hint, the layout kernel cannot tell whether you intended a divider (vertical stack, tap labelled) or a series chain (in-line placement). E15 surfaces the ambiguity as a warning; the kernel falls back to flat placement so nothing is mis-classified silently.
heuristic: Rename the tap net to match the regex (e.g. `VREF`, `SENSE`, `ADC0`) — or add `role: divider` to one of the resistors. Either fixes the ambiguity. If the resistors aren't actually a divider, change the topology so they don't bridge rail to ground. This is a starting point; the kernel's flat-placement fallback is safe, so suppressing E15 is fine when the topology is intentional.
source_of_truth: EPIC-014 frozen decisions — voltage-divider discriminator (IDEA-008 *Open questions*).

rule: Every pin on a `category: transistor` profile must carry a `role:` annotation (`base` / `collector` / `emitter` for BJTs).
explanation: The direction-sensitive kernel rule (ADR-0015) reads `role:` to decide which canonical slot the transistor lands in; an unset role makes the device unplaceable. The check is a forward-looking guard for future BJT-family profiles authored without `role:` — the v1 NPN/PNP profiles ship complete.
heuristic: Add `role: "base" | "collector" | "emitter"` to every pin in the profile's `pins.X` dict, alongside the existing `side` / `type` / `direction` keys. This is a starting point; the role enum is closed in v1, and FET / Darlington additions will extend it.
source_of_truth: EPIC-014 frozen decisions — BJT terminal-role encoding.

rule: Every `category: ic_opamp` instance must connect each `POWER_INPUT`-typed pin to a declared net.
explanation: Op-amp supply pins have no safe floating defaults — an unconnected `V+` or `V-` is a silicon-damage trap that the schematic must surface before render. The check reads the profile's pin-type tag, not the literal pin name, so single-supply op-amp profiles (future work) inherit the rule for free.
heuristic: Add `V+` and `V-` (or whatever your op-amp profile names its supply pins) to the connections list — typically tied to the same `V_PLUS` / `V_MINUS` rails the rest of the analog stage uses. This is a starting point; suppress E17 only when you have a confirmed reason (e.g. an experimental fixture), never silently.

rule: On a `category: ic_timer` instance, prefer the silkscreen-pin form (`T1.1`) over the silicon-name alias (`T1.GND`). Both forms render identically; the silkscreen form is canonical per ADR-0010.
explanation: Mixing both forms in one circuit makes a connection list harder to scan — a reader has to flip between two mental models of the same pin. The validator accepts both forms so the user is never blocked, but the warning encourages a one-form discipline. Style-only; the underlying netlist is identical.
heuristic: Replace each silicon-name reference with the silkscreen-pin form the warning names (`T1.GND` → `T1.1`). This is a starting point; if your team prefers the silicon-name form across the codebase, suppress E18 globally in `meta.erc: { E18: off }`.
source_of_truth: ADR-0010 — MCU profile is dev-board shape.

rule: Every page declared in `pages:` should have at least one placement tagged onto it. A declared-but-empty page renders blank.
explanation: Pages are a *partition* of the slot vocabulary — declaring a page commits the renderer to emitting a blank SVG for it (`<stem>-pN.svg`). A typo in the placement's `page:` tag (e.g. `p3` instead of `p2`) often surfaces as one over-declared and one empty page.
heuristic: Either delete the unused page entry from `pages:` or grep your `.layout.yml` for typos in the `page:` field of placements. This is a starting point; in WIP circuits an empty page is sometimes intentional (placeholder for future work), in which case suppress E19 locally.

rule: Every per-placement `page:` value must name a page declared in the top-level `pages:` block.
explanation: An undeclared page reference is almost always a typo or stale `.layout.yml` that wasn't updated after a `pages:` rename. Without the declaration the renderer has no anchor for the page and the placement falls through to default behaviour, but the schema-level cross-validation catches the same drift.
heuristic: Compare the placement's `page:` value to the `pages:` block and either add the missing page declaration or fix the placement's tag. This is a starting point; the schema-validator's `layout-page-undeclared` check catches the same problem at validate time.

rule: Every cross-page net must have a renderable label anchor on every page it touches. If the kernel could not assign a region to the local-side placement, the off-sheet label is silently dropped and the cross-page reference becomes invisible.
explanation: Cross-page labels (TASK-126) are the only on-sheet trace of a net continuation. When the renderer can't compute an anchor origin (no `region`, no `attached-to` chain), the label is omitted — silently — and a reader of the SVG has no way to know the net continues on another page.
heuristic: Resolve the kernel escalation for the local-side ref (typically by hand-authoring a `free`-slot entry in `.layout.yml`, or by adding a missing component profile). This is a starting point; if the affected pin is intentionally unrouted, suppress E21 on the net via `meta.erc: { <NET>: { E21: off } }`.

rule: Heuristic — more than the configured threshold (default 6) of distinct nets crossing any one page boundary suggests the page split is in the wrong place.
explanation: Cross-page labels are useful but expensive cognitively: every label the reader follows is a context switch. Many cross-page nets between the same two pages usually means a logical block has been split across sheet boundaries by accident. The threshold is a heuristic and tunable.
heuristic: Re-partition the offending placements so the heavily-connected logical block lives on one page. This is a starting point; if the split is intentional (e.g. front-panel vs main board with a known wide interconnect), raise the threshold via `meta.erc.cross-page-threshold` or suppress E22 globally.
